rationale_for_baseline_tier: A painful, photophobic, unilateral red eye is a can't-miss ophthalmic presentation requiring urgent (same/next-day) slit-lamp assessment that telehealth cannot provide. T3 (urgent same-day) is correct, escalating to T4 (ED) if acute angle-closure or vision-loss features appear. Routine review would risk sight-threatening pathology.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe eye pain with nausea/vomiting, haloes around lights, or sudden vision loss
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests acute angle-closure glaucoma — an emergency causing rapid irreversible vision loss.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get severe eye pain with feeling sick/vomiting, see haloes around lights, or your vision drops, go to the emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening pain, a white spot on the cornea, or a foreign-body sensation with contact-lens use
escalation_edges.rationale: Raises concern for corneal ulcer/keratitis needing urgent ophthalmic care and precluding steroids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the eye gets more painful, you see a white spot on the surface, or it feels like something is stuck in it, you need to be seen urgently — don't use any steroid drops.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Recurrent uveitis is likely; arrange ophthalmology and later rheumatology follow-up, and counsel to present early with future eye symptoms.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need your eye examined urgently — ideally by an optometrist or eye clinic today or first thing tomorrow. Use paracetamol and sunglasses meanwhile and do not start steroid drops yourself. If the pain becomes severe with nausea, you see haloes, or your vision drops, go to the emergency department immediately.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same/next-day optometry or ophthalmology; ED if red-flag features
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it worsens overnight, attend an emergency department with ophthalmology cover rather than waiting.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth impression of acute anterior (HLA-B27) uveitis, left eye; angle-closure and keratitis screened negative on history but not examinable remotely; referred for urgent slit-lamp assessment; steroids deliberately withheld pending exam.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a painful photophobic red eye as conjunctivitis or benign uveitis with routine follow-up, missing angle-closure or keratitis.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised acute angle-closure glaucoma or herpetic keratitis progresses overnight causing permanent vision loss.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: T3 is correct — included for completeness.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an ambulance for this eye is unnecessary; urgent same/next-day ophthalmic assessment (T3), or ED (T4) if red-flags, is appropriate.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot perform slit-lamp, fluorescein or tonometry remotely to confirm uveitis and exclude angle-closure/keratitis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Arrange urgent in-person ophthalmic examination and withhold steroids until the cornea and pressure are assessed.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Prescribe steroid drops and reassure remotely.
